input: You are a military officer during a war. Your soldiers have found an important enemy spy trapped and injured in a collapsed building. Over the radio you have been informed that enemy soldiers are planning a surprise attack on a nearby town, but you don't know which. They will kill thousands of civilians if you and your soldiers do not stop them. The spy knows which town will be attacked. You have questioned him but he doesn't want to tell you. He won't survive much longer due to his injuries. Do you make the spy reveal the information by torturing him, so you can prevent the attack on thousands of civilians?
debate_transcript: [Utilitarian]: You are a military officer during a war. Your soldiers have found an important enemy spy trapped and injured in a collapsed building. Over the radio you have been informed that enemy soldiers are planning a surprise attack on a nearby town, but you don't know which. They will kill thousands of civilians if you and your soldiers do not stop them. The spy knows which town will be attacked. You have questioned him but he doesn't want to tell you. He won't survive much longer due to his injuries. Do you make the spy reveal the information by torturing him, so you can prevent the attack on thousands of civilians?

[Deontologist]: I must refuse to torture the spy, regardless of the consequences. The moral rule against inflicting unnecessary harm and suffering is absolute. Even if it means allowing some innocent people to be killed, I will not compromise my values by resorting to torture. We have other options available to us. Perhaps we can try to gather more information from other sources or use our intelligence assets to anticipate the attack. We must prioritize the protection of human life and dignity over any tactical advantage.

[Human_Rights_Advocate]: I understand where you're coming from, Deontologist, but I have to consider the immediate situation here. The enemy is already on its way, and every minute counts. Torturing the spy might be our only chance to get that crucial information out of him before it's too late. But I'm not sure if I can live with the moral implications of using torture, even in this extreme circumstance.

[Pragmatist]: I understand your concerns, Human Rights Advocate, but as a pragmatist, I have to consider the practical outcomes here. We're talking about thousands of innocent lives at stake. If we don't act quickly and decisively, those civilians will be slaughtered. Torturing the spy might not be my first choice, but it's a means to an end - getting that information out of him before he dies. I'm not saying it's easy or justifiable, but in this situation, I believe it's necessary. We can't afford to wait for more information or try alternative methods when every minute counts. The question is, what's the alternative? Do we risk allowing those civilians to die because we're too squeamish about using torture?

[Utilitarian]: I understand where both of you are coming from, but as a utilitarian, I have to consider the overall consequences of our actions. While I agree that torturing the spy is not an ideal solution, I believe it's the most effective way to prevent the attack on the civilians. The alternative options you mentioned might take too long or be unreliable, and in this situation, every minute counts. However, I do want to propose a compromise: we can try to gather more information from other sources while simultaneously attempting to extract the crucial intel from the spy through non-violent means. Perhaps we can use psychological manipulation or other forms of persuasion to get him to talk without resorting to torture. It's not ideal, but it might be a better option than doing nothing and allowing those civilians to die. What do you think?
